name: Via Verde de Coria del Rio to La Puebla del Rio - round trip along the banks of the Guadalquivir River
name_original: Vía Verde de Coria del Río a La Puebla del Río — ida y vuelta por la ribera del Río Guadalquivir
region_intro: Jogging around Coria Del Río offers diverse environments, primarily shaped by its location on the Guadalquivir River. The region features accessible riverside paths, protected wetlands like the Brazo del Este Natural Park, and green corridors such as the Corredor Verde del Río Pudio. Terrain generally includes low elevation changes, with some routes incorporating small hills like Cerro de San Juan, providing varied options for runners. Best jogging routes around Coria Del Río The most popular jogging route is Via Verde de Coria del Rio to La Puebla del Rio - round trip along the banks of the Guadalquivir River, a 10.8 miles (17.3 km) trail that takes 1 hour 54 minutes to complete. This route follows the scenic banks of the Guadalquivir River, offering continuous riverside views. Another top favourite among local runners is Statue of Hasekura Tsunenaga loop from Coria del Río, a moderate 5.2 miles (8.4 km) path. This route includes the cultural landmark of the Hasekura Tsunenaga monument along the riverfront. Local runners also love the Statue of Hasekura Tsunenaga loop from Coria del Río, a 6.7 miles (10.7 km) trail leading through the riverside area, often completed in about 1 hour 11 minutes. Jogging around Coria Del Río is defined by the Guadalquivir River, protected wetland areas, and green corridors. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Coria Del Río are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 2600 runners have used komoot to explore Coria Del Río's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Coria Del Río?
A: Coria Del Río offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 150 trails available for exploration. These routes cater to various fitness levels, including easy, moderate, and difficult options.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for running trails in Coria Del Río?
A: The running trails in Coria Del Río offer a range of difficulty levels. You'll find approximately 11 easy routes, 128 moderate routes, and 19 more challenging trails, ensuring options for all experience levels.

Q: Are there any running routes in Coria Del Río that offer river views?
A: Yes, many running routes in Coria Del Río feature beautiful river views, primarily along the Guadalquivir River. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1014205693/via-verde-de-coria-del-rio-to-la-puebla-del-rio-round-trip-along-the-banks-of-the-guadalquivir-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Via Verde de Coria del Rio to La Puebla del Rio&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the scenic banks of the Guadalquivir, offering continuous riverside vistas.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging paths around Coria Del Río?
A: The terrain around Coria Del Río is diverse, primarily influenced by its riverside location. You can expect accessible riverside paths, routes through protected wetlands like the Brazo del Este Natural Park, and green corridors such as the Corredor Verde del Río Pudio. While generally low in elevation changes, some routes might incorporate small hills like Cerro de San Juan for varied experiences.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running routes in Coria Del Río?
A: Yes, Coria Del Río offers several routes suitable for families. The riverside paths, particularly along the Paseo Carlos de Mesa, are generally flat and accessible, making them ideal for a leisurely run or walk with children. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21849223" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Statue of Hasekura Tsunenaga loop from Coria del Río&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.2-mile path that includes a cultural landmark, which can be an interesting point for families.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Coria Del Río?
A: Many of the natural areas and green corridors around Coria Del Río are dog-friendly, provided your dog is kept on a leash and you clean up after them. The Corredor Verde del Río Pudio and paths within the Brazo del Este Natural Park are generally suitable for running with dogs, offering ample space and natural surroundings.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features I might see while running in Coria Del Río?
A: While running in Coria Del Río, you can encounter several interesting landmarks and natural features. Along the Guadalquivir River, you'll find the Hasekura Tsunenaga monument. The region also boasts the protected Brazo del Este Natural Park, known for its biodiversity, and the scenic Corredor Verde del Río Pudio. For elevated views, Cerro de San Juan offers magnificent panoramas of the town and surrounding areas. You might also spot highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5379343" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hut with views of the lagoon&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5379309" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Dehesa de Abajo Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; in nearby natural areas.

Q: Are there circular running routes available in Coria Del Río?
A: Yes, Coria Del Río features several circular running routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14624042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Isla de los Olivillos – Corta de la Isleta loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a 13.2 km circular path, allowing you to start and finish in the same location without retracing your steps.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in Coria Del Río?
A: The running routes in Coria Del Río are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 40 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse environments, particularly the accessible riverside paths and the serene natural settings found in areas like the Brazo del Este Natural Park and the Corredor Verde del Río Pudio.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Coria Del Río?
A: The best time for jogging in Coria Del Río is typically during the cooler months, from autumn through spring (October to May), when temperatures are more moderate. Summers can be very hot, so if you run during this season, it's advisable to go early in the morning or late in the evening to avoid the peak heat.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by lakes or natural monuments near Coria Del Río?
A: Yes, some routes or nearby areas offer access to lakes and natural monuments. While not directly on every running path, you can find highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5359095" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cañada de los Pájaros&lt;/a&gt; lake or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7365230" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monumental Carob Tree&lt;/a&gt; in María Luisa Park, which could be incorporated into longer runs or visited separately.

Q: Is public transport available to access running trails in Coria Del Río?
A: Coria Del Río is well-connected, and public transport options are available to reach the town. Once in Coria Del Río, many of the riverside paths and local green areas are easily accessible on foot from the town center. For specific trailheads further afield, local bus services might be an option, or you may consider driving.

Q: Are there places to eat or drink near the running routes in Coria Del Río?
A: Yes, Coria Del Río has various cafes, restaurants, and bars, especially along the riverside promenade (Paseo Carlos de Mesa) and in the town center. These establishments offer convenient spots to refuel or relax after your run, with many providing views of the Guadalquivir River.
tips: Nice park where you can walk and eat in the establishments that are there. You can find tributes to Japan (which has a curious link with the municipality). It is located by the river, where the "Toro Nagashi" ceremony is held in summer.
The path that runs along the side of the Río Guadalquivir is close to rice fields and what is called Isla de los Olivillos, on the other side of the river bank.

waypoints.name: View of Isla de los Olivillos
